1: The theme: Elihu's fourth speech continues toward its meteorological climax. Christologically, the speech functions as the human ceiling of wisdom-speech — the most eloquent human defender of divine providence — whose insufficiency establishes the need for the divine Word himself. Colossians 2:3 'in him [Christ] are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' is the answer to all that Elihu approximates but cannot supply.
2: The theme: 'Bear with me a little longer' — the patience Elihu requests is a Christological virtue. James 5:7-11 uses Job's own patience as an example before pointing to 'the Lord's compassion and mercy' (v11). Christ himself is the model of patience under suffering: 1 Peter 2:23 'when they hurled their insults at him, he did not retaliate; when he suffered, he made no threats. Instead, he entrusted himself to him who judges justly.'
3: A revelation of God: 'I will ascribe righteousness (ṣeḏeq) to my Maker.' The assignment of righteousness to God is the ground of Paul's theology: Romans 3:25-26 God demonstrated his righteousness 'so as to be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus.' The divine righteousness that Elihu praises is the righteousness that the NT presents as fully demonstrated at the cross.
4: The theme: Elihu claims to be 'complete in knowledge' — a claim the divine speeches implicitly evaluate. Colossians 2:3 'in Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' is the NT's identification of true completeness of knowledge: it is not a human attribute but a person, available to those who are 'in Christ.'
5: A revelation of God: 'God is mighty and despises no one' — the combination of omnipotence and equal regard. Jesus enacts this combination throughout his ministry: the powerful Son of God who 'has compassion on the crowds' (Matthew 9:36), who does not despise 'one of these little ones' (Matthew 18:10), and who declares 'the one who comes to me I will never drive away' (John 6:37).
6: A revelation of God: 'He does not keep the wicked alive, but gives justice to the poor (ʿăniyyîm).' Christ's ministry specifically fulfills this: Luke 4:18 'he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor'; Matthew 5:3 'blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.' The divine justice for the poor is enacted in the Incarnation — God taking the side of the least.
7: A revelation of God: 'He does not take his eyes off the righteous; he seats them on the throne with kings and exalts them forever.' The eschatological seating with kings is the promise Christ makes: Revelation 3:21 'to the one who is victorious, I will give the right to sit with me on my throne, just as I was victorious and sat down with my Father on his throne.' The exaltation 'forever' (lāneṣaḥ) anticipates the eternal reign of Revelation 22:5.
8: A revelation of God: 'if they are bound in chains, caught in ropes of affliction' — the divine permission of suffering for the righteous. Hebrews 12:5-11 (the 'discipline' passage quoting Proverbs 3:11-12) establishes the same principle: the Father disciplines those he loves. Christ himself is 'the author and perfecter of faith' who 'for the joy set before him endured the cross' (Hebrews 12:2), the supreme demonstration of discipline producing perfection.
9: The theme: 'He shows them what they have done and their transgressions — that they have acted arrogantly.' Divine disclosure of sin's nature is the work of the Holy Spirit whom Christ sends: John 16:8 'when he comes, he will prove the world to be in the wrong about sin and righteousness and judgment.' Conviction of sin is a gift, not condemnation — it opens the way to the mercy Christ provides.
10: A type: 'He opens their ears (gilāh ʾāzĕnām) to correction.' This ear-opening formula is the prophetic revelation formula of Isaiah 50:4-5: 'the Sovereign LORD has given me a well-instructed tongue... he wakens me morning by morning; he wakens my ear to listen like one being instructed. The Sovereign LORD has opened my ears.' This is the Servant's description of his own daily ear-opening by God. Hebrews 5:8 'although he was a Son, he learned obedience from what he suffered' — Christ is the Servant whose ear was opened daily for suffering-instruction.
11: The theme: 'If they listen and serve him, they will complete their days in prosperity.' The condition-promise structure here is not mere retribution but covenant — the relationship between obedience and flourishing that Christ establishes under grace: John 15:10-11 'if you keep my commands, you will remain in my love... I have told you this so that my joy may be in you and that your joy may be complete.'
12: The theme: 'But if they do not listen, they will perish by the sword and die without ever understanding.' The epistemic and physical death of the unresponsive is the consistent OT two-ways teaching. Christ intensifies this: John 8:24 'if you do not believe that I am he, you will indeed die in your sins.' The perishing without understanding is the condition that Christ's teaching addresses.
13: The theme: 'The godless store resentment; they do not cry to God when he binds them.' The failure to pray in affliction is the mark of the godless. Christ teaches the opposite posture: Luke 18:1 'they should always pray and not give up'; Matthew 26:39-44 (Christ prays three times in Gethsemane). The continual prayer in extremity is the model Christ exemplifies and commends.
14: The theme: 'They die young, and their life ends among the cult prostitutes.' The premature death associated with moral dissolution is the negative image of what Christ redeems. 1 Corinthians 6:15-20 addresses the cult-prostitute connection directly in the NT, applying Christ's redemption precisely to sexual sin: 'you were bought at a price; therefore honor God with your bodies' (v20).
15: A type: 'But he rescues the afflicted through their suffering (ʿōnî) and opens their ears through oppression.' The rescue that comes through suffering rather than around it is fulfilled in Christ: Isaiah 53:4-5 'surely he took up our pain and bore our suffering... by his wounds we are healed'; 1 Peter 2:24 'he himself bore our sins in his body on the cross... by his wounds you have been healed.' The rescue is precisely through, not despite, the affliction — the cross as God's method of salvation.
16: A revelation of God: 'He was drawing you from the jaws of distress into a broad place.' The broad place (rĕḥāḇāh) of divine rescue appears throughout the rescue Psalms (Psalms 18:19; Job 31:8; Job 118:5). Christ's resurrection is the ultimate broad-place rescue: 'you have come to Mount Zion, to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem' (Hebrews 12:22) — the spaciousness of God's eschatological city replacing the narrow distress of judgment.
17: The theme: 'But now you are full of the judgment due the wicked.' Elihu sees judgment-signs in Job's suffering. Christ takes on himself 'the judgment due the wicked' in its full weight: 2 Corinthians 5:21 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us.' The judgment that falls on the innocent man — at Calvary as at Job's ash-heap — is the mechanism of the world's rescue.
18: The theme: 'Beware that anger does not lead you into scoffing.' The danger of bitterness under suffering becoming theological mockery is a real spiritual risk. Hebrews 12:15 'see to it that no one misses the grace of God and that no bitter root grows up to cause trouble and defile many.' Christ's grace is the remedy for the bitterness that affliction can generate.
19: The theme: 'Will your wealth hold you in distress?' The insufficiency of material resources before divine judgment is the consistent wisdom teaching. 1 Peter 1:18-19 'you know that it was not with perishable things such as silver or gold that you were redeemed... but with the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or defect.' What wealth cannot purchase, Christ's blood accomplishes.
20: The theme: 'Do not long for the night' — the warning against desiring death as escape. Christ transforms the relationship to death: Philippians 1:21-23 'for me, to live is Christ and to die is gain... I desire to depart and be with Christ, which is better by far.' The death that Job's culture dreads and that Paul desires is a transformed death — not escape but entrance into Christ's presence.
21: The theme: 'Be careful not to turn toward evil, for this is what you have been choosing over enduring affliction.' The temptation to resolve suffering through sin rather than through trust is precisely what Christ resisted in the wilderness temptations (Matthew 4:1-11) and in Gethsemane ('yet not as I will, but as you will,' Matthew 26:39). The faithful endurance of affliction without turning to evil is the path Christ models.
22: A type: 'Behold, God is exalted in his power; who is a teacher (môreh) like him?' The divine teacher-title is fulfilled in Christ. John 13:13-14 'you call me Teacher and Lord, and rightly so, for that is what I am.' Isaiah 30:20 'your Teacher will no longer hide himself; your eyes will see your Teacher' — an eschatological promise that Jesus's disciples see fulfilled in the Incarnation. John 14:26 the Spirit 'will teach you all things and will remind you of everything I have said to you.'
23: A revelation of God: 'Who has prescribed his path for him? Who can say, You have done wrong?' The unchallengeable divine sovereignty is the same that Paul cites in Romans 11:34 (quoting Isaiah 40:13: 'who has known the mind of the Lord?') and 1 Corinthians 2:16 ('who has known the mind of the Lord so as to instruct him? But we have the mind of Christ'). The unanswerable divine freedom is accessible through Christ's revealed mind.
24: The theme: 'Remember to extol his work, which people have sung about through the ages.' The universal hymnic tradition of praising divine works is what the NT church inherits and extends: Revelation 5:9-14 the new song before the Lamb; Revelation 15:3-4 the song of Moses and the Lamb. Christ's redemption is the work that all ages will sing about — the consummate work that surpasses all previous works.
25: A revelation of God: 'All people have seen it; everyone looks at it from a distance.' The universal visibility of divine works is the premise of natural theology: Romans 1:19-20 'since the creation of the world God's invisible qualities — his eternal power and divine nature — have been clearly seen.' But the 'from a distance' acknowledges epistemic limit — the distance is bridged only in Christ (Ephesians 2:13 'you who once were far away have been brought near by the blood of Christ').
26: A revelation of God: 'God is great, beyond knowing (lōʾ-nēḏāʿ); the number of his years cannot be counted.' The divine incomprehensibility and eternity are what the NT applies to Christ: Hebrews 13:8 'Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever'; Revelation 1:8 'I am the Alpha and Omega... who is, and who was, and who is to come, the Almighty.' 1 Timothy 6:16 'who alone is immortal and who lives in unapproachable light' — the unapproachable eternity enters approachable human flesh in the Incarnation.
27: A revelation of God: 'For he draws up the drops of water; they distill as rain from the mist.' The hydrological cycle as divine governance. Colossians 1:17 'in him all things hold together' — the routine mechanics of weather are part of the 'all things' that Christ upholds. Hebrews 1:3 'sustaining all things by his powerful word': the meteorological cycle is sustained word by word.
28: A revelation of God: 'Which the clouds pour down and drop on people in abundance.' The distributive providence of rain. Matthew 5:45 'he causes his sun to rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the righteous and the unrighteous' — Jesus cites rain distribution as evidence of the Father's non-discriminating generosity, the ground for loving enemies. The same rain is now evidence of Christ-the-Creator's ongoing provision.
29: A revelation of God: 'Can anyone understand the spreading of the clouds or the thunder from his canopy (śûkkāh)?' The divine sukkah (canopy/booth) as the vehicle of thunderstorm theophany. Revelation 7:15 'he who sits on the throne will shelter them with his presence (σκηνώσει ἐπ᾽ αὐτούς)' — the same śukkāh/σκηνή vocabulary; Christ's permanent shelter replaces the temporary cloud canopy.
30: A revelation of God: 'He scatters his lightning around him and covers the depths of the sea.' The global reach of divine lightning. Psalms 97:4 'his lightning lights up the world; the earth sees and trembles.' The same Ps 97 is about the LORD's coming to judge: applied to Christ's return in Revelation 1:7 and Job 19:11-16. The lightning that 'lights up the world' is now the Son of Man whose coming is 'like lightning from east to west' (Matthew 24:27).
31: A revelation of God: 'For by these he governs peoples and provides food in abundance.' The dual governance of rain — both judgment and provision. Matthew 6:11 'give us today our daily bread' — Christ teaches prayer for the daily provision that God gives through rain. Revelation 11:6 (the two witnesses who 'have power to shut up the heavens so that it will not rain') shows the same governance continuing eschatologically.
32: A revelation of God: 'He covers his hands with the lightning and commands it to strike its mark.' The lightning wielded by divine hands. Revelation 4:5 'from the throne came flashes of lightning, rumblings and peals of thunder' — the same meteorological signs around the heavenly throne where Christ sits (Revelation 5:6-7). The hands that command lightning are the same hands that bear the marks of the nails (John 20:25-27).
33: A revelation of God: 'Its thunder announces his presence.' The meteorological announcement of divine approach connects to the Sinai theophany (Exodus 19:16-19) and anticipates the eschatological theophany. 1 Thessalonians 4:16 'the Lord himself will come down from heaven, with a loud command, with the voice of the archangel and with the trumpet call of God' — the coming of Christ announced with thunder-level acoustic power.
